coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of a novel carbon dots from red Korean ginseng and its application for Fe2+ sensing and preparation of nanocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Tejwan, Neeraj
Sharma, Anirudh
Thakur, Saloni
Das, Joydeep
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, we have synthesized a novel spherically shaped, water-soluble and green carbon dots (CDs, 2 nm) from a renewable source, aqueous root extract of red Korean ginseng (RKG) via highly efficient microwave irradiation method. The as-synthesized CDs showed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior and therefore, applied for the selective detection of Fe2+ ions via fluorescent quenching method with the detection limit of 0.27 μM. We also investigated the effect of pH and high salt concentration on the fluorescent intensity of CDs. After that, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs, 15 nm) were synthesized using those CDs without adding additional reducing and stabilizing agents. The as-synthesized AgNPs showed high catalytic efficiency in reducing/degrading organic dyes, such as rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO) and congo red (CR) in presence of NaBH4. The pseudo first order rate constants for the reduction of RhB, MO and CR at room temperature were 0.27, 0.26 and 0.18 min−1 respectively. The synthesized AgNPs were also tested for the catalytic reduction of carcinogenic Cr (VI) to non-toxic Cr (III) in presence of formic acid, and ∼85% reduction was obtained within 15 min. All these results clearly demonstrated that the novel green CDs and the CD-derived AgNPs have multiple applications in the field of sensing and catalysis.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of a novel carbon dots from red Korean ginseng and its application for Fe2+ sensing and preparation of nanocatalyst

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, we have synthesized a novel spherically shaped, water-soluble and green carbon dots (CDs, 2 nm) from a renewable source, aqueous root extract of red Korean ginseng (RKG) via highly efficient microwave irradiation method. The as-synthesized CDs showed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior and therefore, applied for the selective detection of Fe2+ ions via fluorescent quenching method with the detection limit of 0.27 μM. We also investigated the effect of pH and high salt concentration on the fluorescent intensity of CDs. After that, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs, 15 nm) were synthesized using those CDs without adding additional reducing and stabilizing agents. The as-synthesized AgNPs showed high catalytic efficiency in reducing/degrading organic dyes, such as rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO) and congo red (CR) in presence of NaBH4. The pseudo first order rate constants for the reduction of RhB, MO and CR at room temperature were 0.27, 0.26 and 0.18 min−1 respectively. The synthesized AgNPs were also tested for the catalytic reduction of carcinogenic Cr (VI) to non-toxic Cr (III) in presence of formic acid, and ∼85% reduction was obtained within 15 min. All these results clearly demonstrated that the novel green CDs and the CD-derived AgNPs have multiple applications in the field of sensing and catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Since its discovery, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted huge interests of nanotechnologists and soon become an important material among the carbon nanofamily. Compared to the frequently used traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have greater biocompatibility, greater photostability, and exhibit tunable emissions upon varied excitation [1–3]. CDs have been reported to possess many incredible applications in the field of sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, and biomedicine [4–6]. The successful use of CDs for sensing various metal ions, such as Fe2+, Cu2+, Au2+, Ag+, Cr6+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Mg2+ and Pb2+ in water has been reported via fluorescence quenching studies [7]. The metal ions can interact with the O and N-containing functional groups on CDs surface, thus favour the electron transfer from the photoexcited CDs to the metal ions and inhibit the radiative electron-hole recombination, leading to significant fluorescence quenching of CDs [8,9]. CDs have also been reported to act as both electron donor as well as electron acceptor; therefore, they can release electrons to electron deficient species and accept electrons from electron rich species [10]. CDs with oxygen-containing functional groups, mostly polyphenolic moieties are used for the synthesis of metal nanoparticles without using addition reducing and stabilizing agents, and those CD-supported metal nanoparticles exhibit superior catalytic properties [11–13]. There are various synthetic methodologies for the preparation of photoluminescent CDs, including electrochemical/chemical oxidation, laser ablation, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, microwave-assisted synthesis, ultrasonic treatment, thermal decomposition, pyrolysis and carbonization [14]. However, recently, green or environmentally friendly synthesis of CDs with superior electronic and optical properties in a large scale from natural bio-resources has become very popular due to their easy availability, low cost, high biocompatibility as well as high renewability [7].

Nowadays, water scarcity, i.e. the lack of pure water resources is one of the major concerns globally. Nearly two million deaths are reported annually due to waterborne disease caused by sewage and impure water sources [15]. The release of untreated household and industrial effluents into the environment caused undesirable ecological changes. Currently, organic dye effluents have recognized as the major threat to aquatic environment, human beings, flora and fauna. Organic dyes, such as methyl orange (MO), congo red (CR) and rhodamine B (RhB) are mostly used in leather, cosmetics, food, paper, plastic, and textile industries, and therefore considered as the major contaminants of industrial wastewater [16]. The bright color of the dyes absorbs the sunlight and reduces the light penetration, thus affect the photosynthesis process and development of aquatic species [17]. Those dye molecules are very stable due to the presence of azo group and their complex structure, and thus hard to degrade biologically. MO is an azo dye which is highly carcinogenic, and causes health hazards like vomiting, nausea, breathing problem, and diarrhea [18]. CR is also belonging to the azo dye family and frequently used in the textile industry. This dye is a potent human carcinogen and causes irritation in skin, eyes and respiratory tract. Besides it can also cause gastrointestinal irritation with nausea, vomiting and diarrhea [19,20]. Like other dyes, RhB is very harmful and causes irritation in skin, eyes and respiratory tract [21]. Therefore, removal/degradation of such organic dyes from wastewater is highly needed. The treatment and removal of organic dyes is a challenging task faced by the industries and environmentalist. Various types of physico-chemical methods like physical adsorption, coagulation, reverse-osmosis, ion exchange, ultrafiltration, chemical oxidation/reduction as well as biological treatment are already in practice for the treatment and remediation of these organic pollutants [22]. However, these methods are ineffective and result into the formation of new compounds which need further treatments. Recently, nanocatalyst-based degradation (oxidative/reductive) of organic dyes is gaining huge attention of the researchers [23–26]. Photocatalytic (oxidative) degradation of toxic pollutants using semiconductor-based nanomaterials, such as TiO2, ZnO, CeO2, etc has widely been used, but the major problem is UV-light is required for their activation as these metal oxides don’t show absorption in visible light region [27–29]. On the other hand, metal nanoparticles (NPs) with their remarkable physico-chemical, optical and electronic properties have been used for the reductive degradation of organic pollutants. Among all, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)-based catalysts exhibit remarkable efficiency towards the catalytic reduction of organic pollutants under visible light [13,30–34]. However, synthesis of AgNPs-based catalysts with high efficiency is a challenging task.

Besides, Cr (VI) is also among the most dangerous pollutant which is generally used in steel fabrication, leather tanning, wood preservatives, metal finishing, and paint formulation, leading to Cr-based water pollution [35]. The oxidation states of Cr play a significant role in its toxicity. Cr (VI) is highly toxic and carcinogenic, whereas Cr (III) is less toxic and required for glucose metabolism in humans [36]. However, the remediation of Cr (VI)-contaminated water is a challenging task. The nanoparticle-based catalytic reduction of Cr (VI) is a promising approach and advantageous than bioremediation of Cr(VI) using bacteria and Cr(VI) removal via selective adsorption (physicochemical method) [37,38].

In this paper, we reported the synthesis of novel water soluble green CDs from a renewable source, aqueous root extract of red Korean ginseng (RKG) by microwave irradiation method for the first time. The principal constituent of RKG is ginsenosides, which have multiple health benefits, including immune-boosting, antidiabetic, antioxidant, anticancer, antiaging, improving brain function, and protection from heart disease [39]. The RKG derived CDs were used as the fluorescent probe for sensing Fe2+ ions in water. After that, we demonstrated the application of our CDs as a reducing as well stabilizing agent to synthesize AgNPs, and those CD-derived AgNPs were further used for the catalytic reduction of organic dyes and Cr(VI) ions.

Silver nitrate, sodium borohydride, rhodamine B (RhB), sodium chloride, calcium chloride, cupric chloride, manganese chloride, cobalt chloride, nickel chloride, ferrous chloride, ammonium chloride, barium chloride, formic acid (HCOOH) and potassium dichromate were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Maharashtra, India). Congo red (CR), cuprous chloride and potassium chloride were purchased from “CDH” Pvt. Ltd. (New Delhi, India). Methyl orange (MO) was obtained from “Merck” Ltd. (Mumbai, Maharashtra). Red Korean ginseng (RKG) used to synthesize CDs was purchased online from amazon.in. Deionized water of conductivity 18 Ω cm− 1, purified by Millipore system has been used in this study.

Ginseng root extract was used to prepare CDs via a facile, economic and straight forward microwave based heating method. A domestic microoven (IFB, India) with frequency of 2500 MHz and power of 700 W was employed for the heating purposes. The extract was heated for 2 mins with 2 min of cooling interval, and the CDs were obtained after 7 consecutive cycles of heating. Then, obtained CDs were purified by making their suspension in water followed by dialysis with 3500 Da MWCO membrane and freeze dried for long time storage.

AgNPs were synthesized via using the aqueous solution of CDs. Briefly, 0.4 mL aqueous solution of RKG derived CDs (112.5 μg/mL) was added into 4 mL of water. The resulting CDs solution was then added to 0.2 mL of aqueous AgNO3 (3.4 mg) solution under stirring condition, and kept under sunlight for 5 min. We have synthesized the AgNPs in the month of December to February during day time between 12.00 pm and 1.00 pm in presence of sunlight, and the intensity of sunlight was around 10,000–15,000 lx. The color of the reaction mixture was changed from colorless to reddish-brown indicating the formation of AgNPs. Similarly, AgNPs were also synthesized using CDs under dark after incubation of 30 min.
Chemically synthesized AgNPs were produced via reduction with NaBH4 in order to compare their catalytic efficiencies with CD-derived AgNPs.

Size and morphology of the prepared nanoparticles were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI; FP 5022/22-Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN USA) operated at 220 kV. Crystallographic structure of synthesized nanoparticles was evaluated by using Rigaku corporation (Smartlab 9 kW, Tokyo, Japan) X-ray powder diffractometer. UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence of the synthesized nanoparticles were analyzed using Thermo Fischer Scientific (Varioskan LUX 3020–561; Waltham, USA) spectrometer. FTIR spectra of the nanoparticles were recorded using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) instrument of Agilent technologies (L1600312; CA, USA).

The QY of the synthesized CDs was calculated by the slope method using quinine sulphate (solvent = 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 0.54) as standard. In order to minimize the effect of reabsorption, the absorbance values were kept under 0.5 at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The following Eq. (1) was used to calculate the QY:

where Q is the quantum yield, K is slope and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” and “st” denote to the unknown sample and standard respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and salt concentration on fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The change in fluorescence intensity of CDs (2 mg/mL) was checked in solution with different pH (pH = 3, 6, 9 and 12) and NaCl concentrations (0.2–1 M). The pH of the solution was adjusted by adding dil. HCl and NaOH solutions.

The sensing of the Fe2+ was performed at room temperature via fluorescence quenching method. Different concentrations of Fe2+ (1–25 μM) were added into 2.5 mg/mL aqueous solution of CDs and kept under dark conditions for 10 min. Then, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The selectivity was checked by adding other metal ions including Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu+ at the concentration of 25 μM. We have also performed the interference experiment to prove the feasibility of the carbon dots for the detection of Fe2+ in presence of various other interfering ions (Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu+). For the interference experiments, 25 μM Fe2+ was added to the 25 μM aqueous solution of different metal ions and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were similarly examined. The limit of detection (LOD) was also calculated by using the formula as shown below:
L O D : 3

Catalytic efficiency of the AgNPs synthesized under sunlight exposure was explored towards the reduction organic azo dyes, such as RhB, CR, and MO using NaBH4. The reactions were carried out in a quartz cuvette in which 3 mL of each dye (0.04 mM) [31] was mixed with freshly prepared 0.25 mL (0.5 M) aqueous solution of NaBH4 and 70 μL AgNPs (0.033 mg). The steady decrease in absorbance intensity at the respective λmax of RhB, CR and MO was recorded at 555 nm, 492 nm and 468 nm respectively using Thermo Scientific Varioskan UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The % catalytic efficiency and the apparent rate constant were calculated by using Eqs. (2) and (3) as shown belowi:

The catalytic reduction of Cr (VI) was evaluated following the protocol of Guo et al. [12]. Briefly, 10 mL of 5 mM Cr (VI) solution, 1 mL HCOOH (98%) and 9 mL distilled water were added into a 50 mL beaker, and pH of the resultant solution was adjusted to 2 by using dil. HCl. After that 500 μL AgNPs (0.24 mg, synthesized under sunlight exposure) solution was added to the reaction mixture, and the absorption was recorded within the wavelength of range 300–400 nm at 3 min intervals. The reduction efficiency of AgNPs was calculated by using the formula:

where Co is the initial absorbance of the Cr(VI) solution and Ct is absorbance at different time intervals.
Each experiment was performed at least 3 times and the results are expressed as mean ± SD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Here we have first prepared CDs using the aqueous root extract of Red Korean Ginseng (RKG) for sensing Fe2+ ions in water, and then utilized the aqueous solution of CDs to synthesize AgNPs as well as investigated their catalytic efficiency (Scheme 1 ). The morphologies of the as-synthesized CDs and AgNPs were checked by TEM analysis which showed nearly spherical shapes of the nanoparticles with size ranges from 1 to 6 nm for CDs ( Fig. 1
a,b). The CDs were more effective in reducing AgNO3 under sunlight exposure, and took only 5 min to complete the reduction process as indicated by changing the color of the solution into reddish-brown ( Fig. 1 c). On the other hand, it took longer time (30 min) to reduce AgNO3 under dark.

The AgNPs synthesized under sunlight exposure displayed nearly spherical shape with size ranges from 10 to 35 nm ( Fig. 2
a). The lattice spacing has also been identified in the TEM images ( Fig. S1 ). The lattice spacing of 0.245 and 0.139 nm denoted (111) and (311) planes of Ag respectively, whereas lattice spacing of 0.281 nm denoted (111) plane of Ag2O crystallites [40–42]. The particle size of AgNPs synthesized under dark was within the range of 10–30 nm with nearly spherical shape (
Fig. S2
).

Fig. 2 b depicted the color of the aqueous solutions of CDs, AgNO3 and AgNPs derived via CDs sunlight exposure. We also performed the EDX analysis to check the elements present in AgNPs synthesized under sunlight exposure ( Fig. 2 c). The results revealed the existence of C, O and Ag in AgNPs. Fig. 2 d displayed the XRD pattern of AgNPs showing several high intensity peaks at 2θ values 27.94°, 32.3°, 38.2°, 44.2°, 46.34°, 54.76°, 57.89°, 64.78°, 67.31°, 76.86° and 86.34°, and a broad peak at 2θ value of 23°. The XRD pattern of AgNPs synthesized in dark also showed similar peaks at the respective 2θ values (
Fig. S3
a). The FTIR spectra of CDs and AgNPs synthesized under sunlight exposure are shown in Fig. 2 e. CDs showed the transmittance peaks at 3650 cm−1, 3253 cm−1, 2923 cm−1, 2894 cm−1, 1712 cm−1, 1625 cm−1, 1591 cm−1, 1366 cm−1, 1211 cm−1, 1014 cm−1, and 1014 cm−1 due to the presence of OH, CH, CO, CC and COC bonds. AgNPs showed all the characteristic peaks of OH, CH, CO stretching, aromatic CC stretching, and COC stretching. FTIR spectra of AgNPs synthesized in dark were also similar to that of AgNPs synthesized under sunlight exposure (
Fig. S3
b).

Fig. 3
a showed the UV–Vis spectra of the aqueous solutions of CDs and AgNPs synthesized under sunlight exposure. CDs displayed a strong absorption band at 285 nm and two weak absorption peaks at 340 nm and 370 nm. AgNPs showed their maximum absorbance near 405 nm. AgNPs synthesized under dark condition also showed maximum absorbance near 400 nm. We have checked the optimum condition of CD concentration and reaction time for the preparation of AgNPs by UV–Vis spectroscopy. 0.4 mL aqueous solution of CDs with different concentrations (20–130 μg/mL) was added to 4 mL distilled water. After that 0.2 mL aqueous solution of AgNO3 (3.4 mg) was added to the resulting CDs solution under stirring condition and kept under sunlight or dark at room temperature. The absorbance at 405 nm for AgNPs was increased with increasing concentration of CD after 5 mins of incubation under sunlight, and the maximum absorption was observed with 112.5 μg/mL concentrations of CDs ( Fig. S4
a). However, no change in the absorbance was observed beyond this concentration, thereby indicating the complete conversion of Ag+ (3.4 mg) into Ag (0). Similarly; with 112.5 μg/mL concentration of CDs, the absorbance for AgNPs was also increased with increasing incubation time, and the maximum absorption was observed following 5 min of incubation under sunlight with no change in absorbance thereafter ( Fig. S4
b). Similarly; under dark condition, 112.5 μg/mL concentrations of CDs was also found to be optimum after 30 min of incubation for the synthesis of AgNPs ( Fig. S4
c,d). Therefore, 112.5 μg/mL of CDs was chosen as optimum for AgNPs synthesis. Further, we have checked the colloidal stability of the as-synthesized AgNPs using UV–Vis spectroscopy. We observed a negligible change in the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak of AgNPs upto 90 days ( Fig. S4
e) after its synthesis, thereby indicating good stability of the as-synthesized AgNPs.

Fig. 3 b depicted the fluorescence spectra of the CDs which exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior when excited within the range of 320–500 nm. Maximum emission was observed near 450–500 nm when excited at 380 nm. The quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 8% taking quinine sulfate as the reference. The change in fluorescence intensity of CDs was also checked under different pH and high salt concentrations. We observed a decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing pH value from 3 to 12 (Fig. 3 c). On the other hand, there was no considerable change in fluorescence intensity in different NaCl concentrations from 0.2 M to 1 M (Fig. 3 d).

Here, we explored the usability of CDs for metal ions sensing in aqueous medium via fluorescence quenching method. At first, we recorded the emission spectra of CDs in presence of various metal ions (25 μM) at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and compared the change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Fig. 4
a showed the ratio of change in fluorescence intensities of CDs in terms of F (fluorescence intensity of CDs after addition of metal ions) to Fo (fluorescence intensity of only CDs). Maximum quenching in the fluorescence intensity was observed in case of Fe2+ as compared with all the tested metal ions (Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, and Cu+). Further, we have also calculated the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe2+ within the concentration range of 0.02–25 μM by plotting the relative change in fluorescence intensity, (Fo–F)/Fo with Fe2+ concentrations. A steady decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs with increasing concentration of Fe2+ ions was observed (Fig. 4 b). (Fo – F)/Fo showed a good linear relationship with the concentrations of Fe2+ in the range of 0.02–25 μM (Fig. 4 c) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.98. The LOD was calculated as 0.27 μM by using the linear regression equation (Fo – F)/Fo = 0.0002xCFe2+ + 0.0185 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. We have further performed the interference experiments to prove the feasibility of the CDs for the detection of Fe2+ (Fig. 4 d). Although the different metal ions (Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, and Cu+) exhibited different degree of quenching on CDs, very little interference was observed from the various metal ions. The quenching effect of Fe2+ was obvious even in presence of the various metal ions, and the difference in the relative fluorescence intensity before and after adding Fe2+ ions was about 50% or more. We have further checked the absorption spectra of CDs in presence of Fe2+ and found that the absorbance values at 340 nm were increased with increasing concentration of Fe2+ ( Fig. S4
f), thereby indicating the formation of stable complex with Fe2+.

The catalytic efficiency of the synthesized AgNPs was tested towards the reductive degradation of three organic dyes, such as RhB, MO and CR in presence of NaBH4 as a reducing agent. RhB exhibited pink color with high intensity absorption peak at 555 nm, and neither the color nor the absorption intensity changed upto 30 mins after the addition of NaBH4. However, in presence of AgNPs as catalyst, both the pink color and absorption intensity at 555 nm of the RhB solution were started to decrease with time due to the catalytic reduction of RhB to its leuco form ( Fig. 5
a). 86% reduction was achieved within 8 min as indicated by the formation of colorless solution ( Fig. 5 b,c). The catalytic degradation reaction of RhB at room temperature followed pseudo first order kinetics with R2 value of 0.98 and the apparent rate constant (kapp) value was 0.27 min−1 (Fig. 5 d).

MO showed a strong orange color in solution with an absorption at 468 nm due to the presence of chromophoric azo (NN) group. It is well known that MO is degraded into4-aminobenzenesulfonateand N, N-dimethyl-benzene-1, 4-diamine following reduction of the azo group. However, we have observed that in presence of NaBH4 alone, the absorption intensity of MO at 468 nm did not change upto 30 min. Meanwhile, in the presence of AgNPs as catalyst, the color became faded and absorption intensity decreased with time indicating the reductive degradation of MO ( Fig. 6
a). 83% reduction took place within 9 min in presence of the catalyst as evident from the formation of colorless solution ( Fig. 6 b,c). The catalytic degradation reaction of MO at room temperature followed pseudo first order kinetics with R2 value of 0.99 and the apparent rate constant (kapp) value was 0.26 min−1 (Fig. 6 d).

CR is a diazo group containing red colored dye and showed two highly intensity peaks at 492 nm and 335 nm. It is well known that CR is degraded intobenzidineand1, 2-diamino-naphthalene-4 sulfonate following reduction of the azo group. NaBH4 alone could not reduce the azo bond as indicated by no change in absorption intensity of CR at 492 nm and 335 nm upto 30 min. Upon addition of AgNPs as catalyst the absorption intensity of the solution at both 492 nm and 335 nm decreased rapidly and became colorless within 10 min ( Fig. 7
a,b). Fig. 7 c depicted that 80% reduction has taken place after 10 min and the reaction followed pseudo first order kinetics with R2 value of 0.99 and the apparent rate constant (kapp) value was 0.18 min−1 (Fig. 7 d).

We have also evaluated the catalytic efficiency of AgNPs towards the reduction of Cr (VI) in presence of HCOOH as reducing agent. The change in absorption intensity of Cr (VI) at 350 nm was measured to monitor the reduction process. We have observed a steady decrease in the intensity of absorption peak at 350 nm and the color of the solution changed from yellow to colorless (Fig. 8
a, b), thereby indicating the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). 85% Cr(VI) reduction has been observed after 15 min (Fig. 8 c). However, the Cr (VI) solution did not show any change in color in absence of AgNPs. The catalytic reduction of Cr(VI) followed pseudo first order kinetics with R2 value of 0.963 and the apparent rate constant (kapp) value was 0.1142 min−1 (Fig. 8 d).

In this current study, we synthesized a novel CDs from the root extract of RKG via microwave irradiation method as it is a simple, low-cost, eco-friendly, least-time consuming, and most effective/efficient method, and evaluated their application for sensing aqueous Fe2+ ions. By this method, CDs with improved quality (uniform size and shape) and quantum yield can be obtained via short-duration heating [7]. The CDs were further utilized to prepare AgNPs as nanocatalysts for the catalytic reduction of organic dyes and Cr (VI).

The as-synthesized nanoparticles were characterized by TEM, EDX, XRD and FTIR techniques. TEM analysis revealed nearly spherical shape of both CDs and AgNPs with size ranging from 1–6 nm and 10–35 nm respectively. CDs can act as both electron donor as well as electron acceptor [10]; therefore, here the reducing property of CDs has been utilized for the synthesis of AgNPs from AgNO3. The major constituent of ginseng is ginsenosides [39] which has several oxygen containing functional groups, such as alcohol as well as ether groups, and monosaccharides, such as aldohexoses. Upon carbonization, carboxylic acid (–COOH) groups may also be formed on the surface of CDs via the oxidation of primary alcohol groups [42,43]. Therefore, here we propose that Ag(I) is first coordinated via the several oxygen-containing functional groups present on the CD surface and reduced thereafter due to the presence of polyphenolic groups. However, the rate of formation of AgNPs in presence of sunlight was higher as compared with dark condition. The enhanced reducing ability of CDs under light is attributed to the semiconductor nature of CDs which produce large number of photogenerated electrons in presence of light [14] as well as the presence of polyphenolic groups on CD surface. The EDX results further revealed the co-existence of carbon, oxygen and silver on AgNPs. XRD pattern also showed the attachment of CDs on AgNPs surface as we observed a broad peak at 2θ value of 23° which is characteristic for CDs [12]. On the other hand, the sharp XRD peaks of AgNPs clearly indicated the presence of highly crystalline Ag and Ag2O phases. The planes observed at 2θ = 38.2°, 44.2°, 64.78°, 76.86° and 86.34° correspond to (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) lattice planes respectively; therefore, indicating the presence of face centred cubic (fcc) structure of Ag [44]. Similarly, the planes observed at 2θ = 27.94°, 32.3°, 46.34°, 54.76°, 57.89°, and 67.31° correspond to (110), (111), (211), (220), (309), and (222) lattice planes respectively; therefore, indicated the presence of hexagonal structure of Ag2O [45]. The FTIR spectra of CDs and CD-derived AgNPs were examined to check the types of functional groups present. CDs exhibited a broad band centred at 3650 and 3253 cm−1 might be due to the presence of OH groups present in carboxylic acids and phenolic OH. The transmittance peaks at 2923 cm−1 and 2893 cm−1 represented CH stretching. A characteristic peak of CO for carboxylic acids was observed at 1712 cm−1. FTIR peaks at 1591 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of aromatic CC and CO bonds. Peak at 1414 cm−1 might be related to C-H bending vibration of CH2 or CH3 groups. Peaks at 1366 cm−1 and 1014 cm−1 might be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric vibration of COC bonds. Peak at 1211 cm−1 might be related to CO stretching vibration of phenols [39,46]. On the other hand, the intensity of CO stretching of carboxylic acid and CO stretching of phenolic moieties decreased significantly in CD-derived AgNPs due to their strong interaction with Ag, and accounts for the stability of the as-synthesized AgNPs in water. However, the intensity of O-H stretching did not decrease probably due to the adsorption of large number of water molecules on AgNPs surface. No specific Ag-O bond stretching at 877 cm−1 was observed might be due to the extensive capping of AgNPs by CDs [44].

The colloidal stability of the AgNPs is attributed to the presence of water-soluble CDs on AgNPs surface as capping agent [46–48]. Here, we propose that Ag(I) is first coordinated via the several oxygen-containing functional groups present on the CD surface, and reduced thereafter to form CD-capped AgNPs and are prevented from agglomeration.

The UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis of CDs showed one strong band at 285 nm due to the π → π* transition of the aromatic CC sp2 domain and two weak absorption peaks at 340 nm and 370 nm due to π → π* and n → π* transitions of CO groups [49]. On the other hand, the as-synthesized AgNPs showed a broad characteristic band centered at 405 nm due to surface plasmon resonance [11]. Besides, CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission when the excitation wavelengths varied from 320 nm to 500 nm, and maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained after excitation at 380 nm. The fluorescence property of the as-synthesized CDs might be attributed to the presence of isolated sp2 carbon clusters and defect states, such as C-related dangling bonds inside the graphitic core [50]. The fluorescence observed under the excitation wavelength of 360–380 nm is originated due to the emission from π *→ π orbitals within the luminescent sp2 carbon clusters, whereas the fluorescence observed above the excitation wavelength of 380 nm is originated due to defect emission [50]. Here, the incorporation of oxygen (O) into the graphitic core [51,52] is responsible for the generation of defect sites. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission or red-shifted emission, and this emission are controlled by the surface states [7]. The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission of CDs is attributed to the oxygen content or presence of surface functional groups like CO on the CD surface. The number of surface defects or trap sites is increased with increasing oxygen content. Excitons are trapped in those defect sites and resulting in the red-shifted emission [7,53,54]. Besides, different sizes of CDs (quantum confinement effect) are also responsible for the observed red-shifted emission [55]. QY of the synthesized CDs was 8% and it is comparable or even higher than many reported CDs obtained from renewable sources [7]. The CDs showed negligible change in fluorescence intensity at very high salt concentration (1 M NaCl). Besides, a steady decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing pH from 3 to 12. The pH dependent change in fluorescence intensity of CDs is attributed to the different deprotonation degrees of the functional groups like OH and COOH present on the surface of CDs at different pH values [56]. The degree of deprotonation decreases with decreasing pH values, and leads to higher surface charge, which confer better hydrophilicity, dispersibility and stability to CDs, and consequently increasing its fluorescence intensity [57]. Another reason for decreasing fluorescence intensity in alkaline medium (pH > 8) is the formation of hydrated product [58].

The fluorescence property of CDs was further applied for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ ions in aqueous solution via fluorescence quenching studies. In our study, Fe2+ ions showed the highest fluorescence quenching ability compared with the other metal ions (Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, and Cu+) tested at a concentration of 25 μM, therefore indicating good selectivity of the CDs for sensing Fe2+ ions. The relative change in fluorescence intensity was also checked upon addition of Fe2+ ions with concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 25 μM in order to determine its sensitivity. The fluorescence intensity of CDs showeda gradual decrease with increasing Fe2+ concentrations, and a linear relationship was obtained between Fe2+ concentrations and fluorescence intensity with LOD value of 0.27 μM. We have also performed the interference experiments to prove the feasibility of the CDs for the detection of Fe2+ in the presence of other interfering metal ions (Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, and Cu+). However, no significant interference was found, thereby indicating a good anti-interference ability of the CDs in detecting Fe2+ in real water sample. The performances of other CD-based sensing agents for detecting Fe2+ ion are present in the Table 1 . Although many research articles have been published about the detection of Fe2+ with CDs, but in most of the studies researchers have used indirect method for the detection of Fe2+ via oxidizing Fe2+ into Fe3+ in presence of oxidizing agent like H2O2 [7]. However; in our present study, the as-synthesized CDs have been used to directly detect Fe2+ without using any oxidizing agent and the obtained LOD value is comparable or even higher than many other reported CDs [53,59–63].

We further checked the absorption spectra of CDs in presence of Fe2+ in order to investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, and found that the absorbance values at 340 nm were increased with increasing concentration of Fe2+ ( Fig. S4
f). Therefore, we propose that the superior sensing performance of our synthesized CDs is attributed to its strong Fe2+ chelating ability due to the presence of large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as, COOH and OH [51]. The strong chelation may favour the electron-transfer from the photoexcited CDs to the conduction band or vacant d-orbital of the Fe2+ ions, and thus inhibit the radiative electron-hole recombination, leading to significant fluorescence quenching [7,51,64,65].

The catalytic efficiency of the CD-derived AgNPs was also tested towards the reduction/degradation of various organic dyes including RhB, MO, and CR in presence of NaBH4 at room temperature. AgNPs and NaBH4 alone did not show any significant reducing ability at the tested concentrations upto 30 min. However, more than 80% dye degradation was observed in presence of both AgNPs and NaBH4 within 10 min. The dye reduction/degradation reactions followed first order kinetics at room temperature with rate constant values of 0.27, 0.26 and 0.18 min−1 for RhB, MO and CR respectively. A comparison of the catalytic efficiencies of various AgNPs nanocatalysts for the reduction/degradation of different dyes is summarized in Table 2 , and the rate constant values for all the tested dyes are in the same order with our prepared nanocatalyst [13,30–34]. The catalytic efficiency of AgNPs can be explained on the basis of electrochemical mechanism in which AgNPs act as an electron relay for oxidant (BH4 −) and reductant (dyes). At first dye molecules and BH4 − ions are simultaneously adsorbed on the AgNPs surface, and then electron transfer occurs from BH4 − ions to the dye molecules through AgNPs. It is believed that BH4 − ions first donate their electrons to AgNPs to form a negatively charged layer over AgNPs, which is subsequently transferred to the dye molecules [31,34]. We have further evaluated the role of CDs on the catalytic reduction/degradation of the dyes via AgNPs, and observed that the CD-derived AgNPs showed significantly higher catalytic efficiency as compared with the NaBH4-derived AgNPs (chemically derived) (Table 3 ). This increased catalytic efficiency of the CD-derived AgNPs is attributed to the presence CDs on AgNPs surface. CDs have excellent electron transfer property, and can help to transfer the electrons from AgNPs surface to the dye molecules [12]. Besides, it has also been observed that AgNPs with higher Ag2O crystallite concentration exhibited higher catalytic efficiency towards the reductive degradation of congo red (CR) dyes in presence of NaBH4 [44]. Therefore, we may propose that Ag2O crystallites are responsible for the excellent catalytic properties of the synthesized AgNPs.

Finally, the CD-derived AgNPs were used for the catalytic reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in presence of HCOOH at room temperature. Neither AgNPs nor HCOOH alone showed any reducing ability at the tested concentrations upto 30 min. However, 85% reduction of Cr(VI) was achieved in presence of both AgNPs nor HCOOH at pH = 2 after 15 min, thereby confirming the catalytic efficiency of AgNPs. The reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr (III) in the solution was further confirmed by the addition of NaOH which turned the solution green via the formation of Hexahydroxochromate. The Cr(VI) reduction reactions followed first order kinetics at room temperature with rate constant values of 0.1142 min−1. So far only limited studies have been conducted for the Cr(VI) reduction using AgNPs, and all the studies have been performed at high temperature [66,67]. Table 4 shows the comparative catalytic efficiencies of several nanocatalysts on the reduction of Cr(VI), and the calculated rate constant value is comparable with the previously reported nanocatalysts or even higher [68–74]. The mechanism of Cr(VI) reduction is proposed as: (i) decomposition of HCOOH molecules into carbon monoxide (CO) and water; (ii) formation of Ag-CO and Ag-CO-Cr(VI) complexes; and (iii) reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) by CO [66]. However, the formation of Ag2O nanocrystallites is reported to decrease the catalytic efficiency of AgNPs towards Cr(VI) reduction [75].

In conclusion, we synthesized water-soluble CDs (2 nm) from a renewable source, red Korean ginseng by microwave irradiation method. CDs show excitation dependent fluorescent behaviour, which is used for the sensing of Fe2+ ions in the linear range of 0.2–25 μM with a detection limit of 0.27 μM. After that, AgNPs with average diameter of 15 nm were synthesized by using those CDs. The AgNPs were further utilized for the catalytic reduction of toxic dyes and Cr(VI) in water with high catalytic efficiency. The catalyst was found to be superior for the safety of the environment against the toxic contaminants and pollutants.
